entry.title: Re: Using IF DATEDIFF() and TODAY() returns no value
entry.content:
@linus_collin23 

Assuming your date format is YYYYMM for e.g. 202501, I tested the below 

INTEGER @MONTHCOUNT

@MONTHCOUNT =DATEDIFF([d/Date] ,TODAY() , "MONTH"), This returns 13 as [d/Date] is smaller (202501) and TODAY is 202602

So in your case IF DATEDIFF([d/Date], TODAY(),"MONTH") <= 0, this will only evaluate TRUE if [d/Date] is bigger than current date so 202603 and beyond.

Below is the test with [d/date] as 202605 and it correctly evaluates to -3

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: Understanding why SM20 Does Not Show All Security Audit Logs
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP Security Audit Log (SAL) is a critical security component used to monitor and record security-relevant activities within SAP systems as depicted below,

 

 

Traditionally, Security Audit Logs are:

  • Configured using SM19

  • Reviewed using SM20

  • Stored in the file system

However, with the evolution of SAP S/4HANA, the Security Audit Log framework has undergone significant architectural changes.

This blog explains the common confusion scenario which I recently faced.

The Scenario

In an S/4HANA we configure the security audit logs by enabling the filters and setting the parameters we require in SM19. After activation, we review logs using SM20.

Observation

Only critical system-level logs, such as - System settings changes and Client settings changes were visible. Other configured events were not appearing in SM20, despite being properly enabled.

At first glance, this felt like some Configuration issue, Incomplete activation, Authorization problem or system malfunction. However, the actual cause was related to the new SAL architecture in S/4HANA.

Root Cause

In S/4HANA systems, SAP introduced new transactions:

  1. RSAU_CONFIG – for Security Audit Log configuration
  2. RSAU_READ_LOG – for reading audit logs

Architecture

Now-a-days, by default the Audit logs are stored in the database instead of the file system.

The relevant configuration can be found in:

  •   RSAU_CONFIG → Parameters → Recording Target

The recording parameter would be set to “Record in Database” to make the Logs to store in database tables.

Behaviour

SM20 can only display logs stored in the file system. It cannot read logs which are stored in the database. As a result, when the recording target is set to database, SM20 may appear to show no results — even though logging is functioning correctly.

Why Does SM20 Still Show Some Logs?

Certain critical events are classified as “permanently active events.” These events are still written to the file system by default. That is why SM20 still displays system-level and client-level configuration changes

SAP_BASIS Version Information

You can use RSAU_CONFIG and RSAU_READ_LOG in any S/4HANA release that includes:

SAP_BASIS 7.50 or higher

Starting from SAP_BASIS 755, the old Security Audit Log environment (originally accessed via SM19 and SM20) is switched off and cannot be used anymore and the system redirects us to the new t-codes

  • SM19 → It calls RSAU_CONFIG

  • SM20 → It calls RSAU_READ_LOG

Conclusion

If SM20 does not show all configured logs in S/4HANA:

  1. Check the Recording Target in RSAU_CONFIG
  2. If set to “Database,” use RSAU_READ_LOG instead of SM20
  3. Understand that SM20 reads only file-based logs

entry.title: How We Built a Scalable Mass Hiring Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM Using SAP BTP
entry.content:
Introduction

In many organizations using SAP SuccessFactors HCM, hiring doesn’t always happen at a steady pace. Sometimes it comes in waves — seasonal hiring, rapid expansion, new project launches — and suddenly HR teams are expected to onboard large numbers of employees in a very short time.

From what I’ve personally experienced, this is when traditional hiring processes start to feel overwhelming.

I’ve seen HR teams juggling spreadsheets, manually entering data, coordinating approvals over emails, and constantly double-checking records to avoid mistakes. For a few hires, this might be manageable. But when the numbers grow, the process becomes stressful and error-prone. Small inconsistencies in pre-hire records can cause issues later in onboarding. Delayed approvals slow everything down. And HR teams end up spending more time fixing data than focusing on people.

What really stood out to me was how exhausting and reactive the process became during high-volume hiring drives. Everyone was working hard — but the system wasn’t working smart.

At the same time, simply trying to “move faster” without structure creates new risks. Speed without governance can impact compliance, reporting accuracy, and overall data integrity. So the real challenge wasn’t just hiring faster — it was hiring smarter.

That’s where the idea for a more streamlined and intelligent solution began.

Our Approach

To address these challenges, we built the Mass and Swift Hiring Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

While working on this solution, my focus was simple: reduce manual effort without losing control. I wanted to create something that HR teams would actually enjoy using — something that feels familiar, intuitive, and aligned with how SuccessFactors already works.

The application provides a clean and structured interface where HR users can manage employee hiring, initiate onboarding for pre-hires, and handle contingent worker hiring in a guided and automated way. Instead of jumping between systems or manually validating data, the process becomes smooth and controlled.

One area I paid special attention to was former employee rehiring. In real-world scenarios, this can get complicated. If not handled correctly, it can disrupt employee history and create reporting inconsistencies. So the solution intelligently detects rehire cases and ensures the correct hiring flow is followed — preserving data integrity while simplifying the process for HR.

Most importantly, automation here doesn’t mean losing governance. Every hiring action still respects validations, approvals, audit requirements, and compliance standards. The difference is that the system now does the heavy lifting.

With this approach, organizations can handle bulk or time-sensitive hiring with much more confidence and far less operational stress.

Key Features and Benefits

1. User- Friendly Hiring Interface

  • SAP Fiori-based responsive UI.
  • Guided steps for data entry, validation, and submission.
  • Supports desktop and mobile access via SAP Build Work Zone.

 

2. Rapid Mass Hiring

  • Create and process multiple hires in a single operation.
  • Preserves and significantly reduces hiring cycle time for bulk recruitment.
  • Ideal for graduate hiring, seasonal hiring, and high-volume recruitment drives.

 

3. Rehire Detection Using Core Employee Data

  • The application performs an automatic rehire check using the candidate’s First Name, Last Name, and Date of Birth, National ID and other attributes.
  • If an existing record is found in SAP SuccessFactors HCM with an inactive status, the system recognizes the individual as a rehire and continues with the rehire process instead of creating a new hire.
  • This helps avoid duplicate employee records and ensures continuity of employee data.

4. Download Standard Hiring Template

  • Provides a predefined, SuccessFactors-aligned template for bulk hiring data entry.
  • Ensures users follow the correct format, mandatory fields, and data structure.
  • Can be easily shared with recruitment partners or external agencies.

 

5. Import Data

  • Allows HR users to import data using structured files (CSV / JSON / XML).
  • Eliminates manual data entry for large hiring volumes.
  • Also allows HR users to manually create a single record directly from the application UI.
  • Supports validation of uploaded data before submission to SAP SuccessFactors.
  • Handles errors gracefully by highlighting invalid or missing fields.

6. Data Validation with Error Highlighting and Logs

  • Performs real-time and pre-processing validation on imported hiring data before submission to SAP SuccessFactors.
  • Validates mandatory fields, data formats, and business rules aligned with SuccessFactors requirements.
  • Highlights validation errors clearly at row and field level, making it easy for users to identify and correct issues.
  • Prevents partial or incorrect data from being processed, ensuring data integrity.

 

7. Download Validation Logs

  • Provides the ability to download detailed error logs for failed records.
  • Error logs include clear reasons for failure (e.g., missing mandatory fields, invalid values).
  • Enables quick correction and re-upload of only failed records.

 

8. Download Successfully Imported Records

  • Successfully imported records can be downloadable.
  • Users can download the list of successfully imported records for confirmation, and audit.
  • Provides full transparency on which records were created successfully in SAP SuccessFactors.

 

9. Create New Onboardee from Scratch

  • Enables HR users to create a new onboardee directly from the application, without relying on pre-hire data.
  • Supports direct entry of onboardee information through a validated UI.
  • Ideal for walk-in hires, urgent hires, contractor conversions, or scenarios where pre-hire data is not available.
  • Ensures all mandatory SuccessFactors fields are captured before submission.
  • Seamlessly creates onboarding records in SAP SuccessFactors using secure OData APIs.

 

10. Simplified Contingent Worker Hiring

  • Enables quick and bulk creation of contingent workers alongside regular hires.
  • Minimizes manual intervention and repetitive HR tasks.
  • Ensures contingent worker data is validated and created consistently in SAP SuccessFactors.

 

Business Value

  • The solution enables organizations to significantly reduce hiring cycle times during high-volume recruitment by streamlining data capture, validations, and record creation for employees and contingent workers.
  • Built-in validations ensure that only complete and consistent data is processed, reducing errors in pre-hire, employee and contingent records and ensuring alignment with SAP SuccessFactors HCM business rules.
  • By simplifying and automating repetitive hiring activities, HR teams can spend less time on transactional work and focus more on strategic workforce planning and talent engagement.
  • Detailed success and failure logs provide clear visibility into hiring outcomes, supporting audits, reconciliation activities, and operational reporting without additional manual effort.
  • The solution supports both full-time and contingent worker hiring through a single framework, allowing organizations to adapt quickly to changing workforce demands.

Conclusion

High-volume hiring can quickly turn into a pressure test for HR teams and systems. I’ve seen how manual processes, fragmented steps, and constant follow-ups can slow down even the most capable teams.

The Mass and Swift Hiring Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM helps remove that pressure by bringing structure, automation, and reliability into the process. It allows organizations to scale hiring without sacrificing data quality, compliance, or control.

For me, this solution is not just about speeding up recruitment. It’s about giving HR teams breathing space — allowing them to focus on people, strategy, and growth instead of repetitive operational tasks.

When hiring becomes both fast and reliable, organizations don’t just fill positions quickly — they build their workforce with confidence.

entry.title: Re: SAP SAC Story Calculation based on Variable like Option
entry.content:
@siva_sapbw 

Create 3 sliders for capturing the 3 thresholds value. Create 3 Script Variables and bind these sliders to them.

 

Now you can use these Script variables in calculation definitions.

Finally create calculated dimension using Use Measure Based dimension to define the text values for each measures 

You can adjust the sliders to tweak the range values accordingly and hide it if required. You could also use an Input Text Box if you are not happy with sliders.

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: A Practical Solution for Multiple Counter Maintenance Plans During SAP PM Data Migration
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

This blog explains how Multiple Counter Maintenance Plans can be uploaded in mass during data migration. We all know that standard tools like LTMC and LSMW is not suitable for Multiple Counter Plans. The upload program is created using a Function Module MPLAN_CREATE. The FM internally creates data in tables such as MPLA, MPOS, and MMPT. The main focus is that we developed in a way that it derives cycle values directly from the cycle set (strategy) and dynamically populating the cycle structure in this FM. This approach made the creation automatic of this complex maintenance plan upload and an alignment with configuration.

BUSINESS REQUIREMENT AND CHALLENGE

We had a business requirement which was to create multiple counter maintenance plans based on cycle sets. During the migration phase, we faced the issue that this requirement proved to be more complex than expected because there is no standard LTMC template available for uploading multiple counter maintenance plans, and LSMW was also not feasible, because it cannot be recorded if there is a scroll as the cycle data becomes difficult to manage when a strategy contains more than four cycle lines. Manual creation was not an option due to the volume of data and the complexity of the cycle definitions.

To address this limitation, we developed a Custom Upload Program using a standard Function Module MPLAN_CREATE. This blog describes how this FM was structured and, how cycle values were automatically derived from the cycle set and populated into the cycle structure of the FM.

MAIN STRUCTURES USED

This Function Module contains the following structures, and we used those structures to develop the upload program. 

  • HEADER – Maintenance plan header
  • ITEM – Maintenance plan items
  • CYCLE – Cycle definition
  • RETURN – Messages

WHY MPLAN_CREATE?

We opted for MPLAN_CREATE because this FM provides full control over Maintenance plan header, Maintenance plan items and Cycle definitions. Instead of hardcoding the cycles, we wanted the system to always read cycle values directly from IP11Z. That way, the upload program would remain stable even if cycle sets change in the future.

OVERALL DESIGN IDEA

The program follows a simple logical flow. That is 

  • First, the maintenance plan header is prepared.
  • Next, the maintenance plan items are prepared.
  • Finally, the cycle data is derived from the cycle set and filled into the Function module cycle table.

INPUT PARAMETERS FOR HEADER

The header structure defines how the plan behaves. We defined only the required scheduling-related fields.

Key fields used in our program:

  • WPTXT – Maintenance plan text
  • ABRHO – Scheduling period
  • MPTYP – Maintenance plan category
  • HUNIT – Unit for Scheduling Period
  • PLAN_SORT – Sort field for maintenance plans

INPUT PARAMETERS FOR MAINTENANCE PLAN ITEMS 

For every maintenance plan there are multiple items linked to the same equipment, but with different task list counters. For every item, we filled the item number, equipment, task list type, task list group, and group counter. Order type, maintenance activity type, and priority were also passed.

One fascinating part here was the work center. The FM does not accept the work center name directly. It accepts the Object ID (OBJID). So, we derived it dynamically by passing the work center and plant into table CRHD and fetching the corresponding OBJID. This value was then assigned to the item structure. The below fields are defined in the item

  • WPPOS – Item number
  • PSTXT – Item text
  • EQUNR – Equipment
  • PLNTY, PLNNR, PLNAL – Task list assignment
  • IWERK – Plant
  • AUART – Order type
  • ILART – Maintenance activity type
  • PRIOK – Priority

WORK CENTER DERIVATION

The work center (GEWRK) cannot be passed directly by its name. So, the work center required by the FM was derived dynamically by using the below logic,

  • Input: Pass Work center (CRHD-ARBL) and Plant (CRHD-WERKS)
  • Output: Fetch the Object ID (CRHD-OBJID)

This OBJID was passed to the MPLAN_CREATE item structure.

READ AND INPUT THE CYCLE SET (STRATEGY)

Our upload program was developed in a way that cycle set is automatically fetched instead of entering cycle values manually. We passed the Cycle set name to table T351P. This table stores the parameters of each strategy, including the cycle values and units.

Input: Cycle Set Name which was created using IP11Z

Table used: T351P (Maintenance Packages)

Data Fetched from T351P

  • ZAEHL – Cycle number
  • ZYKZT – Cycle value
  • ZEIEH – Unit of measure
  • KTEX1 – Short text

By doing this, we ensured that the program always uses the same cycle definition as maintained in IP11Z. For every line found in T351P, one line was created in the MPLAN_CREATE cycle table CYCLE.

The fields were filled as follows:

  • The cycle number was taken from the cycle set sequence.
  • The cycle value was taken from the cycle definition in the cycle set.
  • The unit of measure was taken from the cycle set unit.
  • The cycle text was taken from the cycle set short text.

This mapping paved us to create multiple cycles for the same maintenance plan without manual entry.

Fields populated:

  • NUMBER → From T351P-ZAEHL
  • ZYKL1→ From T351P-KTEX1(fetch the first word, as the value is entered in the first word of the text in cycle set)
  • ZEIEH → From T351P-ZEIEH
  • PAK_TEXT → From T351P-KTEX1

COUNTER POINT DETERMINATION

As this was a counter-based maintenance plan, we had to determine the counter point dynamically. The equipment number was first passed into EQUI table to fetch the object number. That object number was then passed into IMPTT table to fetch the counter point. We selected the counter by filtering the characteristics name which represented the operating hours. This counter point was then assigned to the relevant cycle entries in CYCLE. This step ensured that the cycle was linked to the correct measurement counter of the equipment.

  1. Pass EQUNR → table EQUI → Fetch OBJNR
  2. Pass OBJNR → table IMPTT → Fetch POINT → Filter where ATINN = 'HOURS'

This POINT is passed into CYCLE-POINT.

SEQUENCE AND REPETITION LOGIC

To support multiple counter groups, we used:

  • CYCLESEQIND to define the cycle group
  • SETREPEATIND to indicate repetition

OUTCOME

The outcome of this approach is, 

  • Multiple counter maintenance plans were created automatically
  • Cycle data was always aligned with strategy configuration
  • No dependency on LTMC or LSMW was required
  • Manual creation effort was eliminated

The solution proved to be stable, reusable, and fully controlled.

CONCLUSION

MPLAN_CREATE becomes a practical and powerful alternative when standard migration tools do not support complex PM scenarios such as Multiple Counter Maintenance Plans with cycle sets. By deriving cycle values from the table T351P and counter points from the table IMPTT, we were able to automate the creation of upload program for Multiple Counter Maintenance Plan, while keeping the logic aligned with SAP configuration. This approach helped us to bridge the gap between functional configuration and technical automation in a clean and maintainable way.

entry.title: Re: Confirming quantities with the EAN of the item at EWM...
entry.content:
Good day @Beatriz96 

Thank you for your question.

Barcode scanning is normally not an API topic. It is typically handled by your handheld scanner or mobile client which sends the scanned value into the active input field, like keyboard input. So, the short answer to your question is:

  • No, you do not usually configure an API to open the camera in RF.
  • Yes, you would usually configure the device and the RF presentation device profile so that the scanned Handling Unit is accepted cleanly in the HU field.

The camera button you mentioned is a typical Fiori app pattern, that enables the app to call a scanner feature of your mobile client.

However, RF screens are different because they are designed for FR terminals and scanner devices where you type into the field. So, RF framework allows you to scan or type and then you validate. In your screenshot, the HU field is exactly where the scanner should type the HU number.

Here is a quick guide on what to confirm and do:

1. Confirm you are using the correct user setting for FR. This should look like User à Warehouse number à Resource à (optional) automatic logon and defaults.

In the Maintain User Setting – Radio Frequency app, verify your warehouse is 5550 and your Resource is SHA1-1. This mapping is described in KBA 3672406.

2. In your RF logon you entered DefPressDvc = SE01. That value is your presentation device definition (it controls how RF screens behave on the device, which includes how fields and function keys behave, and it’s part of the RF framework setup). See Using Mobile Devices for more details. You should check:

  • That the device you use is intended for the presentation device you are using.
  • That the input behaviour supports scanning.

3. The functionality of scanning in the HU field should be like this when you are on:

Inbound Process --> Receiving of HUs --> Rec. HU by Delivery. You will get to the screen with the HU input:

  • Tap into the HU field so the cursor is active.
  • Scan the HU barcode with your handheld scanner.
  • The scanner should fill the HU number into the field. Then Enter.
  • This scan or type then Enter flow is how the navigation of RF and RF data entry is defined.

4. Regarding configuration or device setup. The device side is usually where the scanner must act as a keyboard input and send the scanned HU into the focused field. Many scanners can also send a suffix like Enter after scanning (this helps RF processing). So, it is rather a scanner configuration than an API.

5. On the Public Cloud, resource and warehouse mapping is done via Maintain User settings-Radio Frequency and correct RF framework and device setup.

Here are some references to guide you.

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3672406

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3671646

6. If your customer insists on camera scanning, then classic RF is not possible. Rather a Fiori mobile app that support it is required, rather than a classic FR screens.

To summarise:

  • Barcode scanning in RF is normally handled by the device as keyboard input
  • You do not implement API to open the camera in the RF HU field.
  • Ensure you are mapped to the correct warehouse and use the correct RF device.
  • You can scan by placing the cursor in the HU field and validating with Enter.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: “In BI: Is BSEG-KOART sufficient to determine Account field, or must we map BSCHL→TBSL→KOART?”
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I’m trying to reproduce SAP FI document “Account” determination logic (similar to FB03 display) in an external BI/graph analytics solution (ECC).

Background / My understanding

In SAP, an accounting document is identified by the key:

BUKRS (Company Code), BELNR (Document Number), GJAHR (Fiscal Year)

And line items are stored in BSEG.

For “Account” display, my understanding of SAP’s standard concept is:

BSEG has BSCHL (Posting Key) and KOART (Account Type)

Posting Key is configured in TBSL, which also contains KOART (account type) per posting key

Then “Account” shown is chosen depending on KOART (e.g., S→HKONT, D→KUNNR, K→LIFNR, A→ANLN1, M→MATNR)

Key Question

When extracting data to BI, I already have BSEG-KOART stored in the line items.

So instead of reconstructing:
BSEG-BSCHL → join TBSL → get KOART → determine which account field to use,

Can I directly use:
BSEG-KOART (as stored) → determine account field (HKONT/KUNNR/LIFNR/ANLN1/MATNR)

In other words:

Is BSEG-KOART always the “final” account type that SAP already validated at posting time?

Are there any scenarios where relying only on BSEG-KOART could be wrong (e.g., special G/L, alternative account determination, substitutions, document splitting, etc.), such that I must re-check KOART via TBSL?

I want to confirm whether BSEG-KOART alone is sufficient for correct account selection logic in BI (to match SAP’s behavior), or if TBSL-based mapping must be applied for correctness.

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Creating a User Input Form in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Using a Planning Model
entry.content:
Introduction

I've been working with SAC for a while now, and one thing that always bugged me was how overly complicated it felt to do something simple  just let users type in some data and save it. You'd think you need Data Actions for everything, but you really don't.

In this blog, we will build a custom input form in SAC that saves user-entered data directly into a Planning Model using scripting APIs like createMembers() and setUserInput().

Overview

In this solution, we will:

  • Create a Planning Model
  • Define required dimensions, properties, and measures
  • Build a Responsive Story
  • Use a hidden planning table as a technical driver
  • Create a custom form using input fields and a button
  • Use scripting to:
    • Dynamically create dimension members
    • Save data into the model
    • Submit planning data

This approach allows data entry into SAC without using Data Actions.

Requirement

To implement this solution, you need:

  • Access to SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Permissions to create:
    • Planning Models
    • Stories
    • Script Variables
  • Basic understanding of:
    • SAC Planning concepts
    • Story scripting
    • Planning tables

When Would You Actually Use This?

Honestly, this pattern fits a lot of scenarios that don't justify the overhead of Data Actions:

  • HR teams entering employee information during onboarding
  • IT departments registering users or assets
  • Finance teams capturing ad-hoc adjustments
  • Anyone who needs a quick and dirty data collection form inside SAC

It's not meant to replace proper planning workflows with complex business logic. But for straightforward data capture? It does the job really well

Detailed Steps

Step 1: Log in to SAP Analytics Cloud

  • Log in to SAC.
  • From the Navigation Bar, navigate to Stories.

Step 2: Create a Planning Model

First, we create a generic Planning Model.

  • Go to Modeler
  • Click on Model
  • Choose Create New Model
  • Select Create an Empty Model

  • Ensure the model type is Planning Model

Now let’s define:

  • A Dimension (to store user-level records)
  • A Measure/Key Figure (to store numeric data)

👉Let’s say for this example:

  • Dimension Name: User_Id
  • Measure Name: Data         

Create Dimension Properties

Inside the dimension, create properties to store additional details.

Let’s say we create:

  • Email
  • Age
  • Description              

         

Now save the model.

Let’s name the model:

Final Planning Model

Step 3: Create a Responsive Story

  • Go to Stories
  • Click on Responsive Story

This story will act as our form interface.

Step 4: Create a Script Variable

We now create a script variable of type:

  • Planning Model Member

This variable will hold the dynamic member data.

Let’s say we name it:

property

Step 5: Link the Planning Model to the Story

  • Go to Scripting
  • Click the + icon
  • Select Planning Models
  • Add your planning model

Let’s say it appears as:

PlanningModel_1

            

This links the backend planning model to the story.

Step 6: Add a Technical (Hidden) Planning Table

Now we add a planning-enabled table in the story.

This table will act as the technical layer to save data.

Let’s say we name it:

Table_3

In Rows:

Add:

  • The dimension (User_Id)
  • Its properties:
    • Email
    • Age
    • Description

In Columns:

Add:

  • The key figure (Data)

⚠ Important:

  • Enable Unbooked Data

After configuration, hide this table, because it is only used for data submission logic.

Step 7: Create the Input Form

Now create input fields in the story:

Generically:

  • Dimension input
  • Property input fields
  • Measure input field
  • Submit button

Let’s say we create:

  • User ID input → user_ip
  • Email input → email
  • Age input → Age
  • Description input → User_desc
  • Data input → data
  • Submit Button

  •  

Step 8: Create the Dimension Member Dynamically

In the Data input field (edit script), add:

var user_id =user_ip.getValue();   // to get the user_id value
var email_id =email.getValue();   // to get the email value
var age =Age.getValue();     // to get the age value
var user_desc =User_desc.getValue();     // to get the user_desc value

// creating a json to enter the members of the “User_id” field
property = {
	id:user_id,
	description:user_desc,
	properties:{
		
		Email:email_id,
		Age:age
	}
};
// adding the data and creating a new record in the data model.
var check = 	PlanningModel_1.createMembers("User_Id",property);
// checking if the record is created and if it is created then generating a success massage.
if(check){
	Application.showMessage(ApplicationMessageType.Success,"Success");
}

Explanation

  • We first fetch values from form fields.
  • Then we create a JSON structure for the dimension member.
  • createMembers() dynamically creates a new record in the dimension.
  • If successful, we display a success message.

This ensures the member exists before updating measure data.

Step 9: Save the Measure Using setUserInput()

Now, on the Submit Button → onClick event, add:

var data_ip =data.getValue(); // to get the value of the data feild
var user_id =user_ip.getValue();    // to get the newly created user id.

Table_3.getPlanning().setUserInput({"User_Id":user_id},data_ip);
Table_3.getPlanning().submitData();

Explanation

  • Retrieves the entered measure value.
  • Uses setUserInput() to assign value.
  • Calls submitData() to persist data in the model.

⚠ Important:
setUserInput() only works if the member already exists — that’s why we created it earlier.

Final Result

When a user fills the form:

  • A new dimension member is created.
  • Properties are assigned.
  • Measure value is saved.
  • Data is submitted to the planning model.
  • No Data Actions are used.

Conclusion

Using SAC Planning Models and scripting APIs:

  • createMembers()
  • setUserInput()
  • submitData()

We can create powerful custom input forms directly inside SAC.

This solution is:

  • Flexible
  • Lightweight
  • Dynamic
  • Easy to maintain
  • Ideal for simple structured data capture

If you are here, thank you so much for taking out your time to read this. Your feedback will be highly appreciated.

Thanks,
Himanshu

entry.title: How to resolve atc issues
entry.content:
Hi all 
in this blog i have explained how to resolve ATC issues

Introduction:

ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) issues in SAP are quality findings, errors, or warnings flagged during automated code analysis, identifying syntax errors, performance bottlenecks, security flaws, or deviations from best practices in custom ABAP code (Z-code). These issues are crucial for maintaining code quality,, especially during upgrades to S/4HANA. 

ATC  (ABAP Test Cockpit)

let's consider you are getting error in ZOL test system 

step 1: run ATC CHECK 

use SE80 -> search for object -> run ATC check  
or  
use checkman transaction -> select the object type and paste the object -> enter 

step 2: 

We need to check for same error in development system in my case EWE is the development system. 

If the same checkman issue is coming up over there or not, if not then it has been fixed or exempted  
 
then i checked there was no ATC error found  

Step 3: 

In my case no error found in EWE system, then that means error been fixed or exempted 

Next -> check for exemption  

To check -> ATC_SHOW_EXEMPTION -> click on execute -> after click on filter and search for object (as of now no hits found – that means no exemption was raised ) 

If no exemption – then has it be fixed  

step-4 

next we need to check if there were any changes being made against this object (yes in line number 10 they have added t- code but this was missing in ZOL) 

Step-5 

Then go ahead make the changes, try cross porting the changes through SCWB by finding the TR in EWE which had these changes 

In ZOL system 

enter t code SCWB -> click on Sap Note/ Request (pop up will get open)-> select source request -> paste the TR 

to find that TR 
open that object in EWE system -> utilities -> version->version management-> search for related description-> if you get select and click on display or details (check for details) -> if details are matched go back and copy the TR (in this case TR is ER1K8A55CZ) 

 step 6 

enter t code SCWB ZOL system-> click on Sap Note/ Request (pop up will get open)-> select source request -> paste the TR-> copy the TR and paste and select right mark -> then you can see below screen 

there select both rows and click on right mark -> then below screen you can see 

here you need to paste TR (this TR we need to create for system ZOL in which i need to store changes which i have copied in EWE system) 

this TR we need to create through corrective measure 

 step-8 

login in I7P system (to create TR for ZOL system to store chnages) 

select abap corr measures-> click on create->provide details(application component, description,software component,)-> create corrective measure->copy the workbench request 

step-9 
copy workbench request and paste in step 6 

This solves your ATC issue

Conclusion  :

in this blog i have explained how you can resolve ATC by cross porting the TR from the system where that code is working as expected that TR we are cross porting to system where it is not working  (coping TR from working system and pasting in test system) in this way you can resolve your ATC issue or else by raising an exemption you can resolve it 
in this ATC issue T Code was missing that we are cross ported from EWE system.

entry.title: ISO 42001 Certification: The New Benchmark for AI Vendor Selection
entry.content:
Introduction: AI Governance Moves from "Guidelines" to "Certification"

Since the publication of ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System) in December 2023 [1], the landscape of AI governance has evolved steadily. Now, entering 2026, we are witnessing a phase where major certification bodies worldwide have operationalized their audit services, and leading technology vendors are increasingly acquiring this certification. AI governance, which began as a collection of local guidelines and ethical codes, is now adopting a global benchmark: "certification (proof) by a third party."

For SaaS/PaaS vendors like SAP, the questions we receive from customers have shifted. Beyond the traditional functional question of "What can this AI do?", we are now asked non-functional and governance-related questions with equal weight, such as "Is this AI safe?" and "How is the training data managed?"

In this article, from a security and compliance perspective, I will explain the practical implications of the ISO 42001 certification that SAP has acquired and how it can help reduce AI adoption risks for user companies.

1. Why ISO 42001 is the "Common Language of Trust"

Since the widespread adoption of generative AI, companies have faced significant challenges in managing risks such as the "black box" nature of AI (lack of transparency in decision-making) and "hallucinations" (generation of incorrect information). While traditional security standards like ISO/IEC 27001 are essential for information security, they were not designed to fully cover the specific nuances of the AI lifecycle such as data bias, continuous learning, and ethical considerations.

This is why ISO/IEC 42001:2023 was introduced as the first international management system standard specifically for AI.

A key feature of this standard is that it does not certify the accuracy of a specific AI model at a single point in time. Instead, it validates whether an organization has established a "system to continuously identify, manage, and improve AI risks (Management System)." [2] Holding ISO 42001 certification serves as objective proof that a company has built and operates a responsible management structure for its AI systems.

2. SAP's Certification and Scope of Application

SAP announced the acquisition of ISO 42001 certification for its key AI services in 2025.

What is important here is the scope of application. This certification covers core services such as Joule (our generative AI assistant) and SAP AI Core (our AI development and execution platform). This indicates that the applications and development environments utilizing these foundations are managed under a system based on international standards.

Understanding the Difference: "Self-Attestation" vs. "Third-Party Certification"

There are two primary approaches to demonstrating conformity to AI governance: "Self-attestation" by the supplier and "Third-party certification" by an independent body. SAP has chosen the path of "Third-party certification," which involves rigorous audits by an independent organization, to ensure a higher level of objectivity.

For security and legal professionals, this distinction is significant. When adopting a service based solely on self-attestation, user companies often need to send detailed security checklists to vendors and scrutinize their responses—a process that consumes considerable time and resources. However, when a vendor holds ISO 42001 certification, the certification itself serves as objective evidence of governance, which can significantly streamline the Vendor Risk Management (VRM) process.

3. Practical Benefits for User Companies

What are the practical benefits for user companies utilizing SAP's certified environment? I see two main advantages.

1. Clarifying Roles in Supply Chain Risk Management

If a user company were to build an AI service from scratch and aim for ISO 42001 compliance, the effort would be substantial, requiring resources for AI-specific risk assessments and continuous monitoring systems.

By adopting a certified platform (PaaS/SaaS) like SAP Business AI, companies can leverage the vendor's established governance for the infrastructure layer. This concept is similar to the "Shared Responsibility Model" in cloud security.

Specifically, SAP maintains governance over the training environment and infrastructure (Security of the AI). This allows user companies to focus their resources on managing the upper layers directly linked to their specific business values and use cases, such as data quality and ethical decision-making (Security in the AI). This division of labor can help shorten the lead time for AI adoption while maintaining reliability across the supply chain.

2. Supporting Accountability to Stakeholders

In addition to shareholders and regulators, business partners are increasingly conducting strict due diligence regarding the safety and transparency of AI integrated into business operations.

In such scenarios, being able to demonstrate that "we utilize SAP's platform, which complies with the international standard ISO 42001 and is audited by a third party," provides a strong, objective basis for trust. This certification functions as valid evidence to help fulfill accountability requirements to stakeholders.

Furthermore, as legal frameworks like the EU AI Act are implemented globally, compliance is becoming a critical management issue. While ISO 42001 certification does not automatically guarantee legal compliance, it is widely recognized as a framework that supports alignment with these regulations. Leveraging a certified foundation can help streamline the complex process of proving compliance, allowing companies to direct limited resources toward creating business value rather than just defensive measures.

Conclusion: Criteria for Selecting AI in the Future

AI technology evolves daily, but the importance of the trust that underpins it remains constant. As technology advances, the value of transparency and governance only increases.

As a compliance professional, I want to emphasize that for SAP, acquiring ISO 42001 is not a final goal but a milestone. It is objective proof that we have established a robust governance structure to continuously provide an "environment where customers can integrate AI into the core of their business with confidence."

I recommend incorporating "ISO 42001 certification status" into your functional comparison table when selecting AI solutions. This addition will serve as a definitive step toward realizing long-term, stable, and trustworthy AI utilization.

References

  • SAP Trust Center: Certifications and Compliance

entry.title: Unable to capture "Substitute" assignments for email notifications using standard features.
entry.content:
Component : SAP Build Process Automation - Workflow

Problem Description: We are observing a gap between substitute feature of My Inbox and task creation in the My Inbox application via BPA and the custom email notifications triggered by SAP Build Process Automation (BPA) workflows.

Technical Details
Feature in Use: Standard "Substitute" functionality within the My Inbox app.
Issue: When a user defines a substitute in My Inbox, the task is correctly redirected within the inbox UI. However, the custom BPA workflow remains unaware of this change.
Impact: Email notifications generated by the BPA workflow are still sent to the original approver rather than the active substitute.

Question for the Community:
Is there a standard or recommended way to make a BPA workflow aware of an active "My Inbox" substitute for the purpose of sending email notifications?

entry.title: Re: Planned Revenue in Project Financial Booklet SAP S/4H...
entry.content:
Good day @Ayurizkyrs28_ 

Thank you for your question.

There are a few potential root causes to the issue you described. In Public Cloud, Project Financial Booklet does not simply read any plan line with a revenue GL account. Project profiles and planned revenue logic that are used for project revenue scenarios control that functionality.  The issue you face might happen because:

  1. Your project profile is not supported by the Project Financial Booklet app. If your project is investment based for example YP02, you can see plan lines elsewhere, but the booklet will not show the expected content. KBA 3680165 describes this scenario.
  2. Your planned revenue must exist at WBS level and be eligible for revenue scenarios. A common issue here is, i
    1. f your plan revenue was only imported on project level (WBS empty), or
    2. the account you used is not treated as planned revenue in the logic, some apps show the plan line but the “planned revenue” KPI in project apps stays empty.

See the following references for more details:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3635050

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3640693

Below is a quick guide on what to check:

First, you need to confirm your Project Financial Booklet scope and project profile. For that open your Project Financial Booklet-Enterprise Projects app.

Then open your project and confirm it is a project with Revenue (YP05 project Profile). If it is YP02, then that is a strong reason why booklet revenue planning is not shown as you expected.

Secondly, you need to validate the import was done at WBS element level. You can do that in your Import Financial Plan Data App. Check you upload file content and ensure you filled WBS Element for the revenue lines. Add Project/WBS element, GL account (4100040) and Fiscal year. Then confirm the revenue line shows with the WBS element populated. If the WBS is blank, planned revenue may not be applied for WBS based revenue logic.

Third, open your Manage Your Solution, then configure your solution, search for SSCUI 102530 and check whether your revenue account is correctly mapped in the planned revenue determination concept.

See the following references for more details: https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3640693

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3635050

Fourth, check planned revenue and its breakdown in Plan Customer Projects. If you see revenue here but not in the booklet, then it narrows the issue to booklet applicability vs missing planning

See https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3640693 for more details

Finally, ensure your filters in the Project Financial Booklet contain the same period, company code and version. If your planning is not in the same version as your booklet, you will see it in Financial Plan Data but not in the booklet.

To summarise:

  • In Public Cloud, Project Financial Booklet is driven by planned revenue scenario.
  • Booklet will only show planned revenue when the project uses the supported revenue project profile, planned revenue is available at WBS level and aligned with the planned revenue logic used by the Event Based Revenue Recognition.
  • Ensure project Profile is YP05, confirm WBS level planning in the import and validate EBRR planned revenue settings.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Introducing the new pipeline concept in Cloud Integration
entry.content:
@alex_bundschuh , thank you for the helpful blogs. We are planning our migration from SAP PO to SAP Integration Suite and we found a few challenges, we wonder if we overlook the obvious and would like to suggest improvement:

Message Priorization in SAP PO is missing in SAP Integration Suite. A possible solution would be to implement it with the exisiting header JMSPriority. See also: SAP Library - Using Java Message Service, Allow for custom message priorization with header JMSPriority for JMS Adapter and AMQP Adapter.

Technical Acknowledgement (ALEAUD) for Inbound IDoc scenarios. We have to migrate a lot of legacy IDoc interfaces and for monitoring, we return the status (error, success) to the sending SAP backend system. Currently, we only see the option to implement this in the scenario-specific implementation of Pipeline v2 Template Step07 - Outbound Processing. Given the modularity of the pipeline concept and partner directory, we wonder if it would be possible to add a custom-exit 2 before the end message step in Pipeline v2 Generic Step06 - Outbound Processing.

Screenshot A: Current implementation, screenshot B: mockup with custom-exit 2

Philipp

entry.title: Re: "Navigation to this application is not supported." Is...
entry.content:
Dear @taylor_b,

From Public Cloud 2602 onwards, SAP changed the advanced PO creation behavior so that when you click Create Purchase Order from Process Purchase Requisitions app the system navigates into an editable PO screen before the PO is finally ordered/created and SAP introduced/uses an additional navigation/authorization dependency for that step. If that dependency is missing or inactive at the IAM-app level you get exactly the popup: Navigation to this application is not supported

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3705162

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/ee9ee0ca4c3942068ea584d2f929b5b1/72a3e38c35ed4105a69eb5eae750fec2.html

 

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Harnessing SAP BW data product generator for BW modernization with SAP Business Data Cloud

Many of our customers have invested years in building robust SAP BW landscapes.  The question we keep hearing is: how do we modernize without starting from scratch?

In our upcoming webinar, Harnessing SAP BW data product generator for BW modernization with SAP Business Data Cloud, we’ll explore how the SAP BW data product generator, part of SAP Business Data Cloud, helps you transform existing BW assets into reusable data products, quickly and intelligently.

Think of it as turning well-organized shelves in BW into ready-to-serve data packages for cloud innovation.  No heavy lifting.  No wasted value.

We’ll walk through prerequisites, roles, system requirements, plus a live demo showing exactly how to create and activate data products step by step.  And yes, we’ll leave space for real questions in an open Q&A.

If you’re in pre-sales, delivery, or architecture, this session will help you accelerate BW modernization conversations with confidence.

entry.title: Re: Unlocking the True Potential of Data in Files with SAP HANA Database SQL on Files in SAP HANA Cl
entry.content:
Hi @Venky999, 
Yes, you can use this feature in SAP Datasphere. We utilize it in Embedded Object Store (EOS) Spaces and during the installation of Data Products. When you create a Local Table (File) in an EOS Space, a virtual table is set up concurrently. Thus, when sharing this table to a HANA Space, you are essentially sharing access to the virtual table. Similarly, when previewing the data, the virtual table is utilized.

Best
Hannes

entry.title: Re: ATC Execution in HANA 2025 does not show any results
entry.content:
To see why ATC results might not look like you expect, first check that the ATC run actually tried to check the right objects - go to "Monitor and Control Runs" in transaction ATC, find your ATC run there, and look at how many objects ("Total Entities") appear in the "Check Objects" phase. You can look at the exact list of objects checked via "Expert > Display Object Keys".

If there are no or only very few objects there and you are developing in a namespace, the most common pitfall is that the namespace is not set as a Producer namespace in your system. See Registering Name Spaces for how to enable ATC checks for objects in namespaces.

entry.title: Issue with DTW Import – Unexpected Ship To Creation and Change Log Behavior
entry.content:
Good morning,

we are experiencing an issue with one of our customers during the mass import of Business Partner data via DTW.

Specifically, in the DTW file we are only populating the following fields: CardCode and Global Location Number.

During the upload (DTW on table OCRD), SAP, for unknown reasons, automatically creates an empty Ship To address for some Business Partners — for example, 4 out of 15 test uploads — therefore inserting a record into table CRD1.

Furthermore, when checking the SAP change log, for the Business Partners where the Ship To address has been automatically created, the system reports — with today’s date (the DTW execution date) — all the previous changes that had already been recorded in the past.

In practice, SAP appears to “repeat” in the change history, under the current date, the latest modifications previously made, in addition to the actual changes generated by the DTW upload.

Have you ever encountered a similar issue? Do you have any insight into how this behavior could occur?

It is unusual that the system writes into different tables, even when DTW is not involved. Furthermore, there are no empty rows in the import file that could explain the automatic creation of a blank record.

 
Thanks
Chiara

entry.title: Re: "Net Price must be greater than 0 for all items." err...
entry.content:
Dear @R971,

Your situation is standard behavior in Public Cloud:

  • You cannot submit a supplier quotation if any item has net order price = 0 / blank. SAP states explicitly: The system doesn't accept supplier quotations with a net order price equal to 0

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-sourcing-and-procurement/carrying-out-request-for-price-solution-process-steps

  • Partial quotations are not supported in the Manage RFQs process and you cannot delete/remove the unquoted items from the supplier quotation also you can’t set item quantity to 0 to neutralize it

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3600086

So there’s no configuration fix in Public Cloud to allow submit with missing prices. Your 0.01 workaround is exactly what many teams do but as you said, it’s not business-acceptable

Clean ways to handle it (My proposal):

Option A: Create a subset RFQ for only the quoted items

1. In Manage RFQs and Copy your RFQ

2. Remove the items the supplier did not quote and keep only quoted items

3. Publish the RFQ you don’t necessarily need to send it again you can use it to record the response

4. Create/maintain the supplier quotation for this subset RFQ and Submit now every item has a real price so no blocking error

5. Optional add an attachment / note on the new RFQ or quotation referencing the original RFQ number + supplier quoted

So, you’ll have a different RFQ number for the recorded quote but you avoid invented prices

Option B: Raise enhancement request

SAP confirms this limitation and points customers to request an enhancement via SAP Customer Influence

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/2963059/E

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: All that you love to know about SRMPROTOCOL
entry.content:
Please note that the term SRM used here stands for SAP Records Management.

Managing SAP database growth is a constant balancing act between system performance and compliance. If you’ve noticed the SRMPROTOCOL table climbing to the top of your "Top Growth" charts, you aren't alone. With some systems housing billions of records, understanding and managing this table is critical for a healthy SAP environment.

Here is a guide on what SRMPROTOCOL does, how to control it, and how to reclaim your space.

1. Understanding SRMPROTOCOL: The "Who, When, and What"

At its core, the SRMPROTOCOL table functions as the audit trail for SAP Records and Case Management. Whenever a case or record or document in SRM is created, modified, or deleted, the system generates an entry here to maintain a history of activity.

  • Audit Importance: This table is vital for legal and regulatory requirements. It ensures that businesses can track the lifecycle of a document or case for accountability.

  • Data Content: It stores the "SPS ID" (Service Provider Selection ID) and timestamps that link back to specific business objects.

Because these logs track every granular change, the table can grow exponentially in high-volume environments, eventually impacting database performance and backup times.

2. How to Control Log Growth

You don't have to let the table grow indefinitely. SAP provides built-in mechanisms to define how long these logs should live before they are eligible for removal.

Check Your Current Configuration

To see how your system is currently handled, follow these steps:

  1. Execute Transaction SRMREGEDIT.

  2. Locate the SPS ID relevant to your business (you can find these IDs within the SRMPROTOCOL table itself).

  3. Double-click the SPS Element ID.

  4. Navigate to the Classification tab to view the specific logging parameters.

Define "Log Keep Days"

Every organization has different audit requirements. SAP allows you to customize the expiry time for these logs. By setting a specific "retention period," you ensure that the system only flags entries for deletion once they are no longer legally or operationally required.

More details available in the Records Management Logging product documentation page.

3. Cleaning Up and Reducing Space

Once you have defined your retention policies, there are two primary methods to reduce the size of the SRMPROTOCOL table: Deletion or Archiving.

 

FeatureOption A: DeletionOption B: Archiving (Recommended for Compliance)
Primary ToolFM - SRM_PROTOCOL_DELETE_EXPIRY
SAP Note 795613

 If you are using Records Management as a standalone product, t-code ORGANIZER, consider referring to Archiving in Records Management to handle logs related to documents and records. Case Management: If you are using the framework of Case Management, t-code SCASE, consider referring to Archiving in Case Management for logs specifically tied to cases.

Data RetentionData is permanently removed from the system.

Data can be moved to long-term storage (external files).

Best Use CaseWhen logs are past their legal "Keep Days" and no longer needed.When logs must be kept for 5–10 years for audit/legal compliance.
System ImpactImmediate reduction in table row count; requires a "Reorg" to shrink disk files.High initial effort to set up, but provides a sustainable long-term solution.
Accessibility

Logs are deleted forever once the function module is processed.

Logs remain accessible via archive search tools if needed.

Handling Large Volumes

If you have a massive table, do not run the FM online. Instead, wrap the Function Module in a custom report and execute it as a background job to avoid timeouts and system strain.

Archiving is handled within the archiving framework. Each step is ideally scheduled as background job

entry.title: Re: Badi to maintain Attachment as mandatory at SES Level
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

Thank you for your query regarding making attachments mandatory at the Service Entry Sheet (SES) level.

We have reviewed the requirement in SAP S/4HANA Cloud (Public Edition). Currently, there is no released BAdI or standard configuration available that allows enforcing attachments as mandatory at the SES level during Save or Submit.

In the Public Cloud environment, only whitelisted enhancement options are permitted, and attachment validation logic is not exposed through extensibility.

As a possible workaround, you may consider implementing a workflow-based governance process, where approvers verify the presence of attachments and reject the SES if supporting documents are missing. Alternatively, if this requirement is compliance-driven, we recommend submitting an enhancement request via the SAP Customer Influence Portal for future consideration.

Please let us know if you would like guidance on setting up workflow controls.

Kind regards,
Aishwarya

entry.title: Re: Implementation and Customizing of Real-Time Instant P...
entry.content:
Hi all,

The solution that resolved the issue on our side is described below:

First, ensure that the V9 format is implemented in your system. If it is not yet available, please refer to SAP Note 2253571 – Collective information note: CGI_XML_CT, CGI_XML_DD extended with BAdI enhancement.

The following configuration steps were required:

  • DMEE: Update your pain.001.001.09 tree by adding the constant value “INST” in the node
    PmtInf > PmtTpInf > LclInstrm > Cd.

  • FBZP (Country level): Create the relevant payment method and assign the correct format.

  • FBZP (Company Code level): Ensure that the options
    “Single payment for marked item” and “Extended Individual Payment” are both activated.

  • OBPM4: Under Print Parameters, set the indicator “Print Immediately”.

After applying the above settings, the payment file was generated correctly.

I hope this information is helpful for others facing a similar issue.

entry.title: Re: Request for standard production reporting apps
entry.content:
Good day @nelybaf22 

Thank you for your question.

The Public Cloud provides alternatives to the classic list style reports like COOISPI you mentioned. Especially if your users want to see produced quantities and order progress in one place.

The public cloud also enables you to use role based Fiori monitor apps for production and embedded analytics view. You should focus on apps that explicitly supports process orders, not only production orders.

For your requirements I suggest:

You use the Monitor Production Orders or Process app to get a summarised view of your production and order progress, exception and progress monitoring without opening each order one by one. The Manage Production Order or Process Orders gives you the possibility to fix and manage orders that have issues based on the monitoring screen selection.

 

With the Monitor Work Center Schedules, you can focus on your work center with operational overview. You can visualise schedule and workload for each work center and it is useful for daily capacity and execution discussions.

See the following references for more details. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_FIORI/5d441ea8c6ba4ee798d1a679165b3970/f8285e538f0e4510e10000000a441470.html https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2dfa044a255f49e89a3050daf3c61c11/4e366afdf7604bc9b25f39b4aff05cb2.html?locale=en-US

If your users mainly want to know the weekly production, the most robust standalone source is usually material movement based reporting. The Public Cloud offers this function mainly via standard material document analytic apps and filters.

With the Monitor Material Coverage app, you can compare your produced quantity to your PIR. You can compare what was executed and MRP coverage. Therefore, providing you visibility to shortage and coverage. Basically, knowing what production is doing.

To summarise:

  • For your daily operations: use Monitor Production Order or Process Orders
  • For Production quantities: use Material posting based on analytics for actual produced quantity
  • For PIR comparison (optional): use Monitor Material Coverage to show you requirements side, then compare actuals.

This will give you a much more intuitive experience than a single complex list report.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: ConvertExternalUserOnStartDateJob behavior
entry.content:
Hi @angelosuano ,

You need to configure the 'ConvertExternalUserOnStartDateJob' to ensure that an external user is converted to an internal user on the employee’s start date. The start date will be honoured as long as the Time Zone is maintained and available in Job Information as part of the EC setup. If this job is not configured, the user’s state will remain ‘E’ (External), even after completing the MPH step.
 
Regards,
VR

entry.title: Clarification on SAP S/4HANA APIs for Customer, Billing, and Financial History Integration
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am currently working on integrating an external web application with SAP S/4HANA and would appreciate some guidance on selecting the appropriate APIs/services.

Application Context:
My application functions as a customer management portal. In the existing system (Oracle-based), we display and manage:

  • Customer data

  • Bill / Invoice details

  • Bill / Invoice history

  • Financial account (FA) history

  • To-Do management (Add / Update / Delete operations)

We now need to implement similar functionality using SAP S/4HANA.

Clarifications Needed:

  1. Customer Data
    Which APIs are recommended for retrieving and managing customer master data?

  2. Billing / Invoice Details
    Which standard APIs should be used to fetch:

    • Invoice / billing details

    • Invoice history / open items

  3. Financial Account / History
    For financial/account-related history (balances, transactions, etc.), which APIs or services are appropriate?

  4. CRUD Operations
    For operations such as Add / Update / Delete (e.g., To-Dos or related entities), should we rely on:

    • Standard OData APIs

    • CDS-based services

    • Custom APIs

  5. Best Practice Approach
    From an architectural perspective, what is the recommended approach:

    • Standard SAP APIs (API Hub)

    • Custom CDS Views + OData

    • RAP / BTP-based services

Any pointers to relevant APIs, documentation, or best practices would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance for your support.

Best regards,
Kavinkumar

entry.title: Audit Logs Made Simple: Instant Access to the SAP Audit Log Viewer in SAP BTP
entry.content:
Audit logs in SAP BTP for the Cloud Foundry environment are essential for monitoring actions such as creating or updating subaccounts, changing users or roles, or performing actions that affect costs. Account administrators use them to review operational changes, troubleshoot problems, and maintain governance across landscapes. Until now, however, gaining insight into audit logs hasn't always been easy. Several configuration steps were required before you could access audit logs. 

Now managing audit logs in SAP BTP for the Cloud Foundry environment has become significantly more straightforward. Two major updates have been made available that eliminate manual setup and enable you to see audit logs across the entire account hierarchy. 

 

Access to the Audit Log Viewer on the Subaccount Level: Zero Configuration Required 

Previously, viewing audit logs meant working through a four-step process for each subaccount: adding entitlements, subscribing to the service, configuring role collections, and finally accessing the dashboard. That's now a thing of the past. 

The SAP Audit Log Viewer service is now automatically available for every subaccount. This applies to both new subaccounts and all your existing subaccounts. Simply click through from your Subaccount Overview page or use the Actions menu in the Account Explorer, and you're in.  

You no longer need to manually subscribe to the SAP Audit Log Viewer. The entitlement, subscription, and authorization setup happen automatically behind the scenes. Existing subscriptions to the SAP Audit Log Viewer service continue to work. But if you already have any automations or processes built around provisioning the Audit Log Viewer, we recommend that you update your scripts to skip the automatic provisioning of new subscriptions. 

 What this means: less administrative overhead, faster access to the information you need, and one less thing to remember when creating new subaccounts. 



Audit Logs for the Global Account: Finally Visible in the SAP BTP Cockpit  

The second update makes audit logs now accessible directly from the SAP BTP cockpit. Previously, accessing global account audit logs meant either submitting a support ticket or working with APIs. Now, it's as simple as clicking the Audit Log Viewer link in your global account's Actions menu.  

This straightforward access saves time and removes unnecessary barriers. The audit logs provide visibility into critical account-level activities, such as the creation or deletion of subaccounts, user and role assignments, destination management and budget management activities.  

 

These changes reflect a welcome shift toward reducing friction in day-to-day platform management and monitoring. Sometimes the best features are the ones that simply get out of your way.
